title: Sick but Not Dead — Observability Scenario
description: Learn to detect the sick-but-not-dead failure pattern: service still responds (health check = green) but delivers a degraded user experience that traditional monitoring completely misses.

panels.title: 🔴  Row 1 — What Traditional Monitoring Sees (Health Checks Show GREEN)
Storage Cluster Health
panels.description: cluster_health_score: 2 = OK. During sick_storage scenario this stays GREEN while IO is degraded. This is the deception.

panels.title: Ceph OSDs Online
panels.description: All 3 OSDs stay UP during sick_storage scenario. Health check passes. This is the deception.

panels.title: Backend API — Request Rate (req/s)
panels.description: Backend is still receiving and responding to requests during all sick scenarios. Service is NOT dead.

panels.title: The Deception — Why Traditional Monitoring is Blind

options.content:
## Traditional Monitoring Says: ALL GREEN

| Signal | Value | Alert? |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Cluster Health | 2 = OK | ❌ No |
| OSDs Online | 3/3 | ❌ No |
| Backend Request Rate | > 0 | ❌ No |
| Health Check HTTP | 200 | ❌ No |

**Health != Performance.**  
None of these signals detect sick-but-not-dead.  
Scroll down to see what users actually experience.

panels.title: 📊  Row 2 — What Users Actually Experience (The Truth)
Live Error Rate
panels.description: The sick zone: 2–20%. Below 2% = healthy noise. Above 20% = CriticalErrorRate fires. Between 2-20% is the grey zone observability catches but traditional monitoring misses.

panels.title: P99 Latency (All Routes)
panels.description: Tail latency is the sick signal. During sick scenarios p99 can reach 6-8 seconds while p50 stays under 500ms.

panels.title: P99 / P50 Divergence Ratio
panels.description: The bimodal fingerprint. A healthy service has a ratio near 3-4x. Sick-but-not-dead pushes this above 8x because there are two populations: fast (majority) and slow (tail).

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))) / histogram_quantile(0.50, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])))

panels.title: P50 Latency (Median)
panels.description: The misleading signal. P50 stays low during sick scenarios. This is why averages and medians hide the problem. Users in the slow population suffer invisibly.

panels.title: Latency Percentile Fan — P10, P50, P95, P99 Over Time
panels.description: When sick: P10 and P50 look fine. P95 and P99 diverge dramatically. This 'percentile fan' opening wide is the sick-but-not-dead signature in time series. A healthy service has all percentiles close together.

panels.title: 🔬  Row 3 — The Bimodal Fingerprint (How to Detect Sick)
P99/P50 Ratio Over Time — Bimodal Fingerprint (>8 = Sick)
panels.description: When this ratio exceeds 8 it means two latency populations exist simultaneously. The majority of requests are fast (healthy population). A minority is very slow (sick population). Average and median hide the slow minority. This ratio exposes them.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))) / histogram_quantile(0.50, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])))

panels.title: Error Rate Over Time — The Sick Zone (2%–20%)
panels.description: Below 2%: healthy baseline noise. Above 20%: CriticalErrorRate fires (everyone knows). Between 2-20%: the sick zone — service degraded but no critical alert. This is what sick-but-not-dead looks like in error rate.

panels.title: 🌐  Row 4 — API Sick Pattern (Per-Route Breakdown)
Per-Route P99 Latency — /sick vs /ok vs /slow
panels.description: Compare routes directly. /sick shows the log-normal jitter (high p99, low p50). /ok is always fast. /slow is uniformly slow. The contrast teaches why route-level latency is essential — service-wide p99 hides which routes are sick.

panels.title: Per-Route Error Rate — Partial Failure Fingerprint
panels.description: The /sick route has 0% errors (always 200, just slow). The /sick-partial route has ~30% errors (the partial failure pattern). /ok has ~0%. This per-route view is invisible in service-level aggregates — you must break down by route.

panels.title: 💾  Row 5 — Storage Sick Pattern (The Deception)
PVC IO Latency — Elevated During Storage Brownout
panels.description: Activate sick_storage troublemaker scenario and watch these lines rise. Simultaneously the OSD and cluster health panels on the right stay GREEN. The gap between IO latency (sick) and health metrics (green) is the storage brownout signature.

panels.title: Ceph OSDs Online (Stays 3 During sick_storage)
panels.description: This stays GREEN during the brownout. Health check is binary — it checks if OSD processes are running, not if they are serving IO efficiently.

panels.title: Cluster Health Score (Stays 2=OK During sick_storage)
panels.description: cluster_health_score=2 means OK. This stays green during the brownout while IO latency is 8-18x elevated. Health != Performance.

panels.title: The IO Brownout Deception — Why Health Checks Lie

options.content:
## Why Health Checks Miss the Brownout

**Health checks ask:** "Is the OSD process running?"
**Health checks do NOT ask:** "Is the OSD serving IO within latency SLA?"

During `sick_storage`:
- `pvc-db-0` IO latency: **18ms** (normal: 1ms = **18x elevated**)
- `SickStorageBrownout` fires at **>15ms** combined with all OSDs up
- `PVCHighLatency` threshold is **50ms** → **does NOT fire**

The gap between 15ms (sick) and 50ms (traditional alert) is where brownouts live undetected for hours.

panels.title: 🔌  Row 6 — DB / Network Sick Pattern (Pool Exhaustion)
Downstream Call Duration P95 — Frontend → Backend
panels.description: Tracks how long the frontend waits for the backend to respond. During sick_db: p95 rises above 2s as requests queue for a DB pool slot. Backend /health returns 200 immediately (not pool-backed). ConnectionPoolApproachingSaturation alert fires at >2s.

panels.title: Requests In Flight — The Queuing Effect
panels.description: As pool contention grows, requests queue. In-flight count rises above normal. A rising in-flight count with rising p95 latency and LOW CPU usage is the pool saturation signature. CPU saturation looks different: in-flight stays constant, latency rises, CPU pegs at 100%.

panels.title: 📖  Row 7 — Learning Summary: Why Traditional Monitoring Fails
Sick but Not Dead — Learning Reference

options.content:
## Sick but Not Dead — What Each Signal Tells You

| Alert / Signal | What It Detects | Traditional Monitoring | Observability Signal |
|----------------|-----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| `PartialFailureDetected` | Error rate 2–20% sick zone | ❌ Below critical threshold → no alert | ✅ Fires. Service degraded but not dead. |
| `LatencyPercentileDivergence` | p99/p50 ratio > 8 (bimodal) | ❌ Average latency looks OK | ✅ Fires. Two user populations: fast majority + slow tail. |
| `SickAPINotDead` | p95 > 2s while errors < 25% | ❌ Health check still 200 | ✅ Fires. Tail latency + sub-critical errors = sick. |
| `SickStorageBrownout` | IO latency > 15ms with all OSDs up + cluster=OK | ❌ All health checks green, PVCHighLatency (50ms) silent | ✅ Fires. Monitors combination: IO sick + health deceiving. |
| `ConnectionPoolApproachingSaturation` | Downstream call p95 > 2s | ❌ Backend /health returns 200 immediately | ✅ Fires. Pool contention visible in downstream duration. |

## How to Run the Scenarios

```bash
# Trigger API sick pattern manually
curl http://localhost:8080/sick          # log-normal jitter, always 200
curl http://localhost:8080/sick-partial  # 30% HTTP 500

# Trigger storage brownout manually
curl -X POST http://localhost:9200/scenario/sick_storage
# Watch Grafana Row 5 — IO latency rises, OSDs stay green
# Revert:
curl -X POST http://localhost:9200/scenario/healthy

# Let troublemaker run automatically
# sick_api, sick_db, sick_storage rotate in with weights 2, 2, 1
```

## Key Learning Points

1. **Health != Performance.** A service can be running and accepting requests while delivering severely degraded performance.
2. **Averages lie.** P50 (median) hides the slow population. Use P95 and P99. Monitor the **ratio** P99/P50 — a wide ratio means bimodal distribution.
3. **The sick zone (2-20%)** is the detection gap in traditional alert thresholds. Set a `for: 2m` warning at 2% to catch it.
4. **Health checks are not backed by the resource they protect.** `/health` returning 200 proves the process is alive, not that DB connections, IO queues, or downstream services are healthy.
5. **Combine signals for compound detection.** `SickStorageBrownout` only fires when IO is elevated AND health checks are green — the AND is what makes it precise.

## Applying This to Red Hat OpenShift

Same patterns, different instrumentation:
- OCP routes → track p99 per `Route` object not just service
- `etcd` request duration → watch for bimodal distribution (leader election delays)
- Storage: ODF/Ceph health vs `ceph osd perf` IO latency
- Network: pod-to-pod latency via Envoy/Istio proxy metrics, not just Alive/Not-Alive
- Node: CPU throttling (`container_cpu_cfs_throttled_ratio`) vs CPU usage — a container can be healthy but CPU-throttled
